items.correct: Five distinct arguments for God's existence: from motion, causation, contingency, gradation of being, and final causes/teleology — collectively forming Aquinas's natural theology, not a single argument.
items.misconception: Aquinas's Five Ways are five steps of one cosmological argument.
items.random_wrong: Aquinas's Five Ways are five Christian sacraments.
items.why: Each Way is a self-standing argument with different structure. Students treat them as a single chain, missing that Aquinas offered multiple independent routes — the failure of one doesn't undermine the others.

items.q: What does the teleological (design) argument claim?
items.correct: Apparent purpose, order or complexity in the universe implies an intelligent designer — Paley's watchmaker analogy is the classic example.
items.misconception: The design argument proves God created the world in seven days.
items.random_wrong: The design argument proves evolution is false.
items.why: The argument is philosophical, not anti-evolutionary. Many religious thinkers accept evolution as the designer's mechanism — students conflate design with young-earth creationism.

items.q: What is distinctive about the ontological argument?
items.correct: It tries to prove God's existence purely from the concept of God — a priori reasoning needing no empirical evidence about the world.
items.misconception: The ontological argument uses scientific evidence to prove God exists.
items.random_wrong: The ontological argument is the same as the cosmological argument.
items.why: Anselm's argument is famously a priori — pure reasoning from definition. Students try to fit it into evidence-based frameworks, missing what makes it uniquely controversial in philosophy.

items.q: Paley's watch analogy is found in:
items.why: It's Natural Theology. “Dialogues Concerning Natural Religion” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Anselm's formula for God is 'that than which nothing ___ can be conceived'.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: greater. “older” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was Paley's Natural Theology published?
items.why: The real figure is 1802. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
